title: RBA’s dilemma: Fight inflation or save growth?
full_text:
The Australian economy is losing steam even as inflation remains high, and that should ring alarm bells for everyone.
Core trimmed mean inflation for April rose to 3.4 per cent versus 2-3 per cent targeted by the Reserve Bank. But the Westpac–Melbourne Institute Leading Index, which points to likely growth three to nine months ahead, fell to minus 0.17 per cent from 0.11 per cent in March.
It was the first back-to-back below-trend reading in the leading index since late 2024.
A 20 per cent drop in consumer sentiment since late 2025 accounts for nearly all of the 0.32 percentage point deterioration in the index’s growth rate since November.
Westpac’s head of Australian macro forecasting, Matthew Hassan, is concerned that the weakness is becoming more persistent. He expects below-trend growth to carry through the second half of 2026 and into early 2027.
While not signalling an outright collapse – cycle lows are typically below minus 1.5 per cent – the leading index warns that the full impact of the energy price shock and higher interest rates is still working its way through.
Of course there is also another emerging concern – that the sweeping changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax announced in the recent federal budget threatens a further blow to confidence. Some of the fall last month was driven by anxiety over those changes. The concern is that the carnage will worsen now that the measures are confirmed. The ANZ-Roy Morgan consumer confidence index has remained near record lows since the budget was released.
The Reserve Bank board meets over two days on June 15–16 and is widely expected to hit pause on rates. The recent spike in unemployment – jumping to its highest level since November 2021 of 4.5 per cent from 4.3 per cent has sharply cut expectations for further rate rises. Bond markets have responded, with the 10-year yield pulling back to 4.88 per cent after touching a 15-year high of 5.15 per cent.
But the RBA’s dilemma is stark. Inflation remains above its 2-3 per cent target. Government spending is running at 27 per cent of GDP – the highest in 30 years outside the Covid-19 period. While primarily driven by rising bond yields globally, the recent rise in Australia’s 10-year bond yield to a 15-year high of 5.15 per cent was partly due to concern Australia’s public spending will remain at a colossal 27 per cent of GDP – the highest in 30 years apart from the pandemic – for years to come, according to Bell Potter head of institutional sales and trading, Richard Coppleson.
Bond yields have since retreated, partly because Australia’s unemployment rate unexpectedly jumped to a 4½-year high of 4.5 per cent.
That’s hardly good news for the economy.
“Right now the market is thinking they will hold and wait,” Coppleson says of the RBA. “But if inflation stays high, or worse goes higher, they have no choice but to raise rates.” The budget’s proposed tax changes have made a difficult picture worse. The planned overhaul of capital gains tax and negative gearing has unnerved investors and drawn a warning from Macquarie Group chief economist Ric Deverell. He says the measures add to his conviction there will be “little to no real price growth over the coming decade or two, with the risk now that we are on the cusp of another significant fall” in national real property prices.
Markets have been voting with their feet. Retail stocks have been savaged. Temple & Webster is down 77 per cent over the past nine months. Accent Group has lost 76 per cent in 4½ months, hitting a 13-year low. Myer is down 59 per cent and Harvey Norman off 40 per cent over nine months. JB Hi-Fi, Nick Scali, Premier Investments and Super Retail have all shed between 37 per cent and 43 per cent.
Coppleson says the market is starting to price in a slowdown. “Retail stocks have been savaged,” he says. Tax-loss selling from institutional investors is expected to dominate on the stockmarket until the end of the financial year. Retail investors typically do their tax-loss selling in the final two weeks of the financial year.
A Newspoll conducted after the budget found 47 per cent of voters believe it will be bad for the economy, against just 22 per cent who think it will be good. It has been labelled the worst budget for the economy in 33 years.
The fiscal picture also raises longer-term concerns. Gross debt is projected to exceed $1 trillion in 2026–27 and could approach $1.3 trillion if left unchecked, raising fears the annual interest bill could eventually exceed the cost of the NDIS and the age pension combined.
Critics argue the budget redistributes wealth rather than grows it. There are fears that if higher-income earners face reduced returns, they will spend less – weakening the economy and pushing up unemployment.
The start-up community has been particularly vocal with founders warning that lifting the effective capital gains tax rate will prompt the best talent to relocate to lower-tax jurisdictions such as Singapore or New Zealand, taking future tax revenue with them.
For now, the RBA is in a holding pattern. But with inflation sticky, government spending high, and sentiment sliding, the next move could come sooner than the market now expects. As Coppleson puts it: “Once inflation is entrenched, it’s hard to exorcise.”
